# itertools

The concrete iterator types.

### itertools

Path: `/docs/rust-itertools/pkg-itertools-itertools.html`

Extra iterator adaptors, functions and macros.

Extra iterator adaptors, functions and macros. To extend [ Iterator ] with methods in this crate, import the [ Itertools ] trait: Copy # #[allow(unused_imports)] use itertools::Itertools; Now, new methods like interleave are available on all iterators: Copy use itertools::Itertools; let it = (1..3).interleave(vec![-1, -2]); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec![1, -1, 2, -2]); Most iterator methods are also provided as functions (with the benefit that they convert parameters using [ IntoIterator ]): Copy use itertools::interleave; for elt in interleave(&[1, 2, 3], &[2, 3, 4]) { /* loop body */ # let _ = elt; } Crate Features use_std Enabled by default. Disable to compile itertools using #![no_std] . This disables any item that depend on allocations (see the use_alloc feature) and hash maps (like unique , counts , into_grouping_map and more). use_alloc Enabled by default. Enables any item that depend on allocations (like chunk_by , kmerge , join and many more). Rust Version This version of itertools requires Rust 1.63.0 or later. Modules § Macros § Structs § impl BorrowMut for StripPrefixError borrow_mut fn borrow_mut (self: &mut Self ) -> &mut T impl CloneToUninit for StripPrefixError clone_to_uninit unsafe fn clone_to_uninit (self: &Self , dest: *mut u8 ) impl Into for StripPrefixError into fn into (self: Self ) -> U Calls U::from(self) . That is, this conversion is whatever the implementation of From for U chooses to do. impl From for StripPrefixError from fn from (t: T ) -> T Returns the argument unchanged. impl TryInto for StripPrefixError Error try_into fn try_into (self: Self ) -> Result >::Error> impl TryFrom for StripPrefixError Error try_from fn try_from (value: U ) -> Result >::Error> impl Any for StripPrefixError type_id fn type_id (self: &Self ) -> TypeId impl ToOwned for StripPrefixError Owned to_owned fn to_owned (self: &Self ) -> T clone_into fn clone_into (self: &Self , target: &mut T ) impl Debug for StripPrefixError Source fmt fn fmt (self: &Self , f: &mut $crate::fmt::Formatter ) -> $crate::fmt::Result impl Clone for StripPrefixError Source clone fn clone (self: &Self ) -> StripPrefixError impl Send for StripPrefixError impl Sync for StripPrefixError impl Freeze for StripPrefixError impl UnsafeUnpin for StripPrefixError impl Unpin for StripPrefixError impl UnwindSafe for StripPrefixError impl RefUnwindSafe for StripPrefixError impl IntoEither for StripPrefixError Enums § Source is_done fn is_done (self: &Self ) -> bool Return true if self is Done , false if it is Continue . Trait Implementations § impl Borrow for FoldWhile borrow fn borrow (self: &Self ) -> &T impl BorrowMut for FoldWhile borrow_mut fn borrow_mut (self: &mut Self ) -> &mut T impl CloneToUninit for FoldWhile clone_to_uninit unsafe fn clone_to_uninit (self: &Self , dest: *mut u8 ) impl Into for FoldWhile into fn into (self: Self ) -> U Calls U::from(self) . That is, this conversion is whatever the implementation of From for U chooses to do. impl From for FoldWhile from fn from (t: T ) -> T Returns the argument unchanged. impl TryInto for FoldWhile Error try_into fn try_into (self: Self ) -> Result >::Error> impl TryFrom for FoldWhile Error try_from fn try_from (value: U ) -> Result >::Error> impl Any for FoldWhile type_id fn type_id (self: &Self ) -> TypeId impl ToOwned for FoldWhile Owned to_owned fn to_owned (self: &Self ) -> T clone_into fn clone_into (self: &Self , target: &mut T ) impl Clone for FoldWhile Source clone fn clone (self: &Self ) -> FoldWhile impl Debug for FoldWhile Source fmt fn fmt (self: &Self , f: &mut $crate::fmt::Formatter ) -> $crate::fmt::Result impl PartialEq for FoldWhile Source eq fn eq (self: &Self , other: &FoldWhile ) -> bool impl Send for FoldWhile impl Sync for FoldWhile impl Freeze for FoldWhile impl UnsafeUnpin for FoldWhile impl Unpin for FoldWhile impl UnwindSafe for FoldWhile impl RefUnwindSafe for FoldWhile impl IntoEither for FoldWhile impl Copy for FoldWhile impl Eq for FoldWhile impl StructuralPartialEq for FoldWhile Traits § Source interleave_shortest fn interleave_shortest (self: Self , other: J ) -> InterleaveShortest ::IntoIter> Alternate elements from two iterators until at least one of them has run out. Iterator element type is Self::Item . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let it = (1..7).interleave_shortest(vec! -1, -2 ); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! 1, -1, 2, -2, 3 ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let it = ( 1 .. 7 ) . interleave_shortest ( vec! [ - 1 , - 2 ]); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [ 1 , - 1 , 2 , - 2 , 3 ]); Run Source intersperse fn intersperse (self: Self , element: ::Item ) -> Intersperse An iterator adaptor to insert a particular value between each element of the adapted iterator. Iterator element type is Self::Item . This iterator is fused . Copy use itertools::Itertools; itertools::assert_equal((0..3).intersperse(8), vec! 0, 8, 1, 8, 2 ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; itertools :: assert_equal (( 0 .. 3 ) . intersperse ( 8 ), vec! [ 0 , 8 , 1 , 8 , 2 ]); Run Source intersperse_with fn intersperse_with (self: Self , element: F ) -> IntersperseWith An iterator adaptor to insert a particular value created by a function between each element of the adapted iterator. Iterator element type is Self::Item . This iterator is fused . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let mut i = 10; itertools::assert_equal((0..3).intersperse_with(|| { i -= 1; i }), vec! 0, 9, 1, 8, 2 ); assert_eq!(i, 8); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let mut i = 10 ; itertools :: assert_equal (( 0 .. 3 ) . intersperse_with ( || { i -= 1 ; i }), vec! [ 0 , 9 , 1 , 8 , 2 ]); assert_eq! ( i , 8 ); Run Source get fn get (self: Self , index: R ) -> ::Output Returns an iterator over a subsection of the iterator. Works similarly to slice::get . Panics for ranges ..=usize::MAX and 0..=usize::MAX . It's a generalisation of Iterator::take and Iterator::skip , and uses these under the hood. Therefore, the resulting iterator is: ExactSizeIterator if the adapted iterator is ExactSizeIterator . DoubleEndedIterator if the adapted iterator is DoubleEndedIterator and ExactSizeIterator . Unspecified Behavior The result of indexing with an exhausted core::ops::RangeInclusive is unspecified. Examples Copy use itertools::Itertools; let vec = vec! 3, 1, 4, 1, 5 ; let mut range: Vec = vec.iter().get(1..=3).copied().collect(); assert_eq!(&range, & 1, 4, 1 ); // It works with other types of ranges, too range = vec.iter().get(..2).copied().collect(); assert_eq!(&range, & 3, 1 ); range = vec.iter().get(0..1).copied().collect(); assert_eq!(&range, & 3 ); range = vec.iter().get(2..).copied().collect(); assert_eq!(&range, & 4, 1, 5 ); range = vec.iter().get(..=2).copied().collect(); assert_eq!(&range, & 3, 1, 4 ); range = vec.iter().get(..).copied().collect(); assert_eq!(range, vec); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let vec = vec! [ 3 , 1 , 4 , 1 , 5 ]; let mut range : Vec &#x3C; _ > = vec . iter () . get ( 1 ..= 3 ) . copied () . collect (); assert_eq! ( &#x26; range , &#x26; [ 1 , 4 , 1 ]); // It works with other types of ranges, too range = vec . iter () . get ( .. 2 ) . copied () . collect (); assert_eq! ( &#x26; range , &#x26; [ 3 , 1 ]); range = vec . iter () . get ( 0 .. 1 ) . copied () . collect (); assert_eq! ( &#x26; range , &#x26; [ 3 ]); range = vec . iter () . get ( 2 .. ) . copied () . collect (); assert_eq! ( &#x26; range , &#x26; [ 4 , 1 , 5 ]); range = vec . iter () . get ( ..= 2 ) . copied () . collect (); assert_eq! ( &#x26; range , &#x26; [ 3 , 1 , 4 ]); range = vec . iter () . get ( .. ) . copied () . collect (); assert_eq! ( range , vec ); Run Source zip_longest fn zip_longest (self: Self , other: J ) -> ZipLongest ::IntoIter> Create an iterator which iterates over both this and the specified iterator simultaneously, yielding pairs of two optional elements. This iterator is fused . As long as neither input iterator is exhausted yet, it yields two values via EitherOrBoth::Both . When the parameter iterator is exhausted, it only yields a value from the self iterator via EitherOrBoth::Left . When the self iterator is exhausted, it only yields a value from the parameter iterator via EitherOrBoth::Right . When both iterators return None , all further invocations of .next() will return None . Iterator element type is EitherOrBoth . Copy use itertools :: EitherOrBoth :: { Both , Right }; use itertools :: Itertools ; let it = ( 0 .. 1 ) . zip_longest ( 1 .. 3 ); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! code title = "unresolved intra-doc link" > Both ( 0 , 1 ), Right ( 2 ) / code >); Copy use itertools :: EitherOrBoth :: { Both , Right }; use itertools :: Itertools ; let it = ( 0 .. 1 ) . zip_longest ( 1 .. 3 ); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [ Both ( 0 , 1 ), Right ( 2 )]); Run Source zip_eq fn zip_eq (self: Self , other: J ) -> ZipEq ::IntoIter> Create an iterator which iterates over both this and the specified iterator simultaneously, yielding pairs of elements. Panics if the iterators reach an end and they are not of equal lengths. Examples Copy use itertools::Itertools; let a = vec! 1, 2 ; let b = vec! 3, 4 ; let zipped: Vec = a.into_iter().zip_eq(b.into_iter()).collect(); itertools::assert_equal(zipped, vec! (1, 3), (2, 4) ); Copy use itertools::Itertools; let a = 1, 2 ; let b = 3, 4, 5 ; // This example panics because the iterators are not of equal length. let _zipped: Vec = a.iter().zip_eq(b.iter()).collect(); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let a = vec! [ 1 , 2 ]; let b = vec! [ 3 , 4 ]; let zipped : Vec &#x3C; _ > = a . into_iter () . zip_eq ( b . into_iter ()) . collect (); itertools :: assert_equal ( zipped , vec! [( 1 , 3 ), ( 2 , 4 )]); Run should_panic Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let a = [ 1 , 2 ]; let b = [ 3 , 4 , 5 ]; // This example panics because the iterators are not of equal length. let _zipped : Vec &#x3C; _ > = a . iter () . zip_eq ( b . iter ()) . collect (); Run Source batching fn batching (self: Self , f: F ) -> Batching A “meta iterator adaptor”. Its closure receives a reference to the iterator and may pick off as many elements as it likes, to produce the next iterator element. Iterator element type is B . Copy use itertools::Itertools; // An adaptor that gathers elements in pairs let pit = (0..4).batching(|it| { match it.next() { None => None, Some(x) => match it.next() { None => None, Some(y) => Some((x, y)), } } }); itertools::assert_equal(pit, vec! (0, 1), (2, 3) ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; // An adaptor that gathers elements in pairs let pit = ( 0 .. 4 ) . batching ( | it | { match it . next () { None => None , Some ( x ) => match it . next () { None => None , Some ( y ) => Some (( x , y )), } } }); itertools :: assert_equal ( pit , vec! [( 0 , 1 ), ( 2 , 3 )]); Run Source chunk_by fn chunk_by (self: Self , key: F ) -> ChunkBy Return an iterable that can group iterator elements. Consecutive elements that map to the same key (“runs”), are assigned to the same group. ChunkBy is the storage for the lazy grouping operation. If the groups are consumed in order, or if each group's iterator is dropped without keeping it around, then ChunkBy uses no allocations. It needs allocations only if several group iterators are alive at the same time. This type implements IntoIterator (it is not an iterator itself), because the group iterators need to borrow from this value. It should be stored in a local variable or temporary and iterated. Iterator element type is (K, Group) : the group's key and the group iterator. Copy use itertools::Itertools; // chunk data into runs of larger than zero or not. let data = vec! 1, 3, -2, -2, 1, 0, 1, 2 ; // chunks: |---->|------>|--------->| // Note: The `&` is significant here, `ChunkBy` is iterable // only by reference. You can also call `.into_iter()` explicitly. let mut data_grouped = Vec::new(); for (key, chunk) in &data.into_iter().chunk_by(|elt| *elt >= 0) { data_grouped.push((key, chunk.collect())); } assert_eq!(data_grouped, vec! (true, vec![1, 3 ), (false, vec! -2, -2 ), (true, vec! 1, 0, 1, 2 )]); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; // chunk data into runs of larger than zero or not. let data = vec! [ 1 , 3 , - 2 , - 2 , 1 , 0 , 1 , 2 ]; // chunks: |---->|------>|--------->| // Note: The `&#x26;` is significant here, `ChunkBy` is iterable // only by reference. You can also call `.into_iter()` explicitly. let mut data_grouped = Vec :: new (); for ( key , chunk ) in &#x26; data . into_iter () . chunk_by ( | elt | * elt >= 0 ) { data_grouped . push (( key , chunk . collect ())); } assert_eq! ( data_grouped , vec! [( true , vec! [ 1 , 3 ]), ( false , vec! [ - 2 , - 2 ]), ( true , vec! [ 1 , 0 , 1 , 2 ])]); Run Source group_by fn group_by (self: Self , key: F ) -> ChunkBy 👎 Deprecated since 0.13.0: Use .chunk_by() instead See .chunk_by() . Source chunks fn chunks (self: Self , size: usize ) -> IntoChunks Return an iterable that can chunk the iterator. Yield subiterators (chunks) that each yield a fixed number elements, determined by size . The last chunk will be shorter if there aren't enough elements. IntoChunks is based on ChunkBy : it is iterable (implements IntoIterator , not Iterator ), and it only buffers if several chunk iterators are alive at the same time. Iterator element type is Chunk , each chunk's iterator. Panics if size is 0. Examples Copy use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! 1, 1, 2, -2, 6, 0, 3, 1 ; //chunk size=3 |------->|-------->|--->| // Note: The `&` is significant here, `IntoChunks` is iterable // only by reference. You can also call `.into_iter()` explicitly. for chunk in &data.into_iter().chunks(3) { // Check that the sum of each chunk is 4. assert_eq!(4, chunk.sum()); } Copy use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! 1, 2, 3 ; // Panics because chunk size is 0. let _chunks = data.into_iter().chunks(0); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ 1 , 1 , 2 , - 2 , 6 , 0 , 3 , 1 ]; //chunk size=3 |------->|-------->|--->| // Note: The `&#x26;` is significant here, `IntoChunks` is iterable // only by reference. You can also call `.into_iter()` explicitly. for chunk in &#x26; data . into_iter () . chunks ( 3 ) { // Check that the sum of each chunk is 4. assert_eq! ( 4 , chunk . sum ()); } Run should_panic Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ 1 , 2 , 3 ]; // Panics because chunk size is 0. let _chunks = data . into_iter () . chunks ( 0 ); Run Source tuple_windows fn tuple_windows (self: Self ) -> TupleWindows Return an iterator over all contiguous windows producing tuples of a specific size (up to 12). tuple_windows clones the iterator elements so that they can be part of successive windows, this makes it most suited for iterators of references and other values that are cheap to copy. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let mut v = Vec::new(); // pairwise iteration for (a, b) in (1..5).tuple_windows() { v.push((a, b)); } assert_eq!(v, vec! (1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 4) ); let mut it = (1..5).tuple_windows(); assert_eq!(Some((1, 2, 3)), it.next()); assert_eq!(Some((2, 3, 4)), it.next()); assert_eq!(None, it.next()); // this requires a type hint let it = (1..5).tuple_windows:: (); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! (1, 2, 3), (2, 3, 4) ); // you can also specify the complete type use itertools::TupleWindows; use std::ops::Range; let it: TupleWindows , (u32, u32, u32)> = (1..5).tuple_windows(); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! (1, 2, 3), (2, 3, 4) ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let mut v = Vec :: new (); // pairwise iteration for ( a , b ) in ( 1 .. 5 ) . tuple_windows () { v . push (( a , b )); } assert_eq! ( v , vec! [( 1 , 2 ), ( 2 , 3 ), ( 3 , 4 )]); let mut it = ( 1 .. 5 ) . tuple_windows (); assert_eq! ( Some (( 1 , 2 , 3 )), it . next ()); assert_eq! ( Some (( 2 , 3 , 4 )), it . next ()); assert_eq! ( None , it . next ()); // this requires a type hint let it = ( 1 .. 5 ) . tuple_windows :: &#x3C;( _ , _ , _ )>(); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [( 1 , 2 , 3 ), ( 2 , 3 , 4 )]); // you can also specify the complete type use itertools :: TupleWindows ; use std :: ops :: Range ; let it : TupleWindows &#x3C; Range &#x3C; u32 >, ( u32 , u32 , u32 )> = ( 1 .. 5 ) . tuple_windows (); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [( 1 , 2 , 3 ), ( 2 , 3 , 4 )]); Run Source circular_tuple_windows fn circular_tuple_windows (self: Self ) -> CircularTupleWindows Return an iterator over all windows, wrapping back to the first elements when the window would otherwise exceed the length of the iterator, producing tuples of a specific size (up to 12). circular_tuple_windows clones the iterator elements so that they can be part of successive windows, this makes it most suited for iterators of references and other values that are cheap to copy. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let mut v = Vec::new(); for (a, b) in (1..5).circular_tuple_windows() { v.push((a, b)); } assert_eq!(v, vec! (1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 4), (4, 1) ); let mut it = (1..5).circular_tuple_windows(); assert_eq!(Some((1, 2, 3)), it.next()); assert_eq!(Some((2, 3, 4)), it.next()); assert_eq!(Some((3, 4, 1)), it.next()); assert_eq!(Some((4, 1, 2)), it.next()); assert_eq!(None, it.next()); // this requires a type hint let it = (1..5).circular_tuple_windows:: (); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! (1, 2, 3), (2, 3, 4), (3, 4, 1), (4, 1, 2) ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let mut v = Vec :: new (); for ( a , b ) in ( 1 .. 5 ) . circular_tuple_windows () { v . push (( a , b )); } assert_eq! ( v , vec! [( 1 , 2 ), ( 2 , 3 ), ( 3 , 4 ), ( 4 , 1 )]); let mut it = ( 1 .. 5 ) . circular_tuple_windows (); assert_eq! ( Some (( 1 , 2 , 3 )), it . next ()); assert_eq! ( Some (( 2 , 3 , 4 )), it . next ()); assert_eq! ( Some (( 3 , 4 , 1 )), it . next ()); assert_eq! ( Some (( 4 , 1 , 2 )), it . next ()); assert_eq! ( None , it . next ()); // this requires a type hint let it = ( 1 .. 5 ) . circular_tuple_windows :: &#x3C;( _ , _ , _ )>(); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [( 1 , 2 , 3 ), ( 2 , 3 , 4 ), ( 3 , 4 , 1 ), ( 4 , 1 , 2 )]); Run Source tuples fn tuples (self: Self ) -> Tuples Return an iterator that groups the items in tuples of a specific size (up to 12). See also the method .next_tuple() . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let mut v = Vec::new(); for (a, b) in (1..5).tuples() { v.push((a, b)); } assert_eq!(v, vec! (1, 2), (3, 4) ); let mut it = (1..7).tuples(); assert_eq!(Some((1, 2, 3)), it.next()); assert_eq!(Some((4, 5, 6)), it.next()); assert_eq!(None, it.next()); // this requires a type hint let it = (1..7).tuples:: (); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! (1, 2, 3), (4, 5, 6) ); // you can also specify the complete type use itertools::Tuples; use std::ops::Range; let it: Tuples , (u32, u32, u32)> = (1..7).tuples(); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! (1, 2, 3), (4, 5, 6) ); See also Tuples::into_buffer . Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let mut v = Vec :: new (); for ( a , b ) in ( 1 .. 5 ) . tuples () { v . push (( a , b )); } assert_eq! ( v , vec! [( 1 , 2 ), ( 3 , 4 )]); let mut it = ( 1 .. 7 ) . tuples (); assert_eq! ( Some (( 1 , 2 , 3 )), it . next ()); assert_eq! ( Some (( 4 , 5 , 6 )), it . next ()); assert_eq! ( None , it . next ()); // this requires a type hint let it = ( 1 .. 7 ) . tuples :: &#x3C;( _ , _ , _ )>(); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [( 1 , 2 , 3 ), ( 4 , 5 , 6 )]); // you can also specify the complete type use itertools :: Tuples ; use std :: ops :: Range ; let it : Tuples &#x3C; Range &#x3C; u32 >, ( u32 , u32 , u32 )> = ( 1 .. 7 ) . tuples (); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [( 1 , 2 , 3 ), ( 4 , 5 , 6 )]); Run Source array_windows fn array_windows (self: Self ) -> ArrayWindows Return an iterator over all contiguous windows, producing arrays of size N . array_windows clones the iterator elements so that they can be part of successive windows. This makes it most suited for iterators of references and other values that are cheap to copy. If the input iterator contains fewer than N items, no windows are returned. Otherwise, if the input iterator contains k items, exactly k+N-1 windows are returned. (This formula still applies when N==0 , and means that k+1 zero-length windows are returned for k input items.) Copy use itertools::Itertools; // Three-element windows from the items 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 . itertools::assert_equal( (1..6).array_windows:: (), vec! [1, 2, 3 , 2, 3, 4 , 3, 4, 5 ] ); // When the input list is shorter than the window size, no windows // are returned at all. let mut windows = (1..6).array_windows:: (); assert_eq!(None, windows.next()); // When the window size is zero, one more window is returned // than there are items. itertools::assert_equal((1..6).array_windows:: (), vec! [ ; 6]); // In some cases you don't have to specify the window size // explicitly with a type hint, because Rust can infer it for a, b, c in (1..6).array_windows() { println!("{a} {b} {c}"); } Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; // Three-element windows from the items [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. itertools :: assert_equal ( ( 1 .. 6 ) . array_windows :: &#x3C; 3 >(), vec! [[ 1 , 2 , 3 ], [ 2 , 3 , 4 ], [ 3 , 4 , 5 ]] ); // When the input list is shorter than the window size, no windows // are returned at all. let mut windows = ( 1 .. 6 ) . array_windows :: &#x3C; 10 >(); assert_eq! ( None , windows . next ()); // When the window size is zero, one more window is returned // than there are items. itertools :: assert_equal (( 1 .. 6 ) . array_windows :: &#x3C; 0 >(), vec! [[]; 6 ]); // In some cases you don't have to specify the window size // explicitly with a type hint, because Rust can infer it for [ a , b , c ] in ( 1 .. 6 ) . array_windows () { println! ( "{a} {b} {c}" ); } Run Source circular_array_windows fn circular_array_windows (self: Self ) -> CircularArrayWindows Return an iterator over all windows, wrapping back to the first elements when the window would otherwise exceed the length of the iterator, producing arrays of size N . circular_array_windows clones the iterator elements so that they can be part of successive windows, this makes it most suited for iterators of references and other values that are cheap to copy. One window is returned per element of the input iterator. This is true even if the input contains fewer elements than the window size. In that situation, input elements are repeated within each window. The results are as if the input had been treated as a cyclic list, and a window of N items had been returned for every starting point in the cycle. (If the window size is zero, the function still returns one empty window per element of the input iterator.) Copy use itertools::Itertools; // Three-element windows from 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 , with two of // them wrapping round from 5 to 1. itertools::assert_equal( (1..6).circular_array_windows:: (), vec! [1, 2, 3 , 2, 3, 4 , 3, 4, 5 , 4, 5, 1 , 5, 1, 2 ] ); // If the input is shorter than the window size, input // items are repeated even within the same window. itertools::assert_equal( (1..3).circular_array_windows:: (), vec! [1, 2, 1, 2, 1 , 2, 1, 2, 1, 2 ] ); // If the input contains only one item, the returned window // repeats it N times. let once = std::iter::once(1); itertools::assert_equal( once.circular_array_windows:: (), vec! [1, 1, 1 ] ); // If the input is empty, no windows are returned at all. let empty = std::iter::empty:: (); let mut windows = empty.circular_array_windows:: (); assert_eq!(None, windows.next()); // If the input is empty, no windows are returned at all. let empty = std::iter::empty:: (); let mut windows = empty.circular_array_windows:: (); assert_eq!(None, windows.next()); // One window is returned per item, even if the windows are empty. itertools::assert_equal( (1..6).circular_array_windows:: (), vec! [ ; 5] ); // In some cases you don't have to specify the window size // explicitly with a type hint, because Rust can infer it. for a, b, c in (1..10).circular_array_windows() { println!("{a} {b} {c}"); } Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; // Three-element windows from [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], with two of // them wrapping round from 5 to 1. itertools :: assert_equal ( ( 1 .. 6 ) . circular_array_windows :: &#x3C; 3 >(), vec! [[ 1 , 2 , 3 ], [ 2 , 3 , 4 ], [ 3 , 4 , 5 ], [ 4 , 5 , 1 ], [ 5 , 1 , 2 ]] ); // If the input is shorter than the window size, input // items are repeated even within the same window. itertools :: assert_equal ( ( 1 .. 3 ) . circular_array_windows :: &#x3C; 5 >(), vec! [[ 1 , 2 , 1 , 2 , 1 ], [ 2 , 1 , 2 , 1 , 2 ]] ); // If the input contains only one item, the returned window // repeats it N times. let once = std :: iter :: once ( 1 ); itertools :: assert_equal ( once . circular_array_windows :: &#x3C; 3 >(), vec! [[ 1 , 1 , 1 ]] ); // If the input is empty, no windows are returned at all. let empty = std :: iter :: empty :: &#x3C; i32 >(); let mut windows = empty . circular_array_windows :: &#x3C; 5 >(); assert_eq! ( None , windows . next ()); // If the input is empty, no windows are returned at all. let empty = std :: iter :: empty :: &#x3C; i32 >(); let mut windows = empty . circular_array_windows :: &#x3C; 5 >(); assert_eq! ( None , windows . next ()); // One window is returned per item, even if the windows are empty. itertools :: assert_equal ( ( 1 .. 6 ) . circular_array_windows :: &#x3C; 0 >(), vec! [[]; 5 ] ); // In some cases you don't have to specify the window size // explicitly with a type hint, because Rust can infer it. for [ a , b , c ] in ( 1 .. 10 ) . circular_array_windows () { println! ( "{a} {b} {c}" ); } Run Source tee fn tee (self: Self ) -> (Tee , Tee ) Split into an iterator pair that both yield all elements from the original iterator. Note: If the iterator is cloneable, prefer using that instead of using this method. Cloning is likely to be more efficient. Iterator element type is Self::Item . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let xs = vec! 0, 1, 2, 3 ; let (mut t1, t2) = xs.into_iter().tee(); itertools::assert_equal(t1.next(), Some(0)); itertools::assert_equal(t2, 0..4); itertools::assert_equal(t1, 1..4); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let xs = vec! [ 0 , 1 , 2 , 3 ]; let ( mut t1 , t2 ) = xs . into_iter () . tee (); itertools :: assert_equal ( t1 . next (), Some ( 0 )); itertools :: assert_equal ( t2 , 0 .. 4 ); itertools :: assert_equal ( t1 , 1 .. 4 ); Run Source map_into fn map_into (self: Self ) -> MapInto Convert each item of the iterator using the Into trait. Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; assert_eq! ( ( 1 i32 .. 4 i32 ) . map_into :: f64 >() . collect_vec (), vec! code title = "unresolved intra-doc link" > 1 f64 , 2 f64 , 3 f64 / code > ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; assert_eq! ( ( 1 i32 .. 4 i32 ) . map_into :: &#x3C; f64 >() . collect_vec (), vec! [ 1 f64 , 2 f64 , 3 f64 ] ); Run Source map_ok fn map_ok (self: Self , f: F ) -> MapOk Return an iterator adaptor that applies the provided closure to every Result::Ok value. Result::Err values are unchanged. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let input = vec! Ok(41), Err(false), Ok(11) ; let it = input.into_iter().map_ok(|i| i + 1); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! Ok(42), Err(false), Ok(12) ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let input = vec! [ Ok ( 41 ), Err ( false ), Ok ( 11 )]; let it = input . into_iter () . map_ok ( | i | i + 1 ); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [ Ok ( 42 ), Err ( false ), Ok ( 12 )]); Run Source filter_ok fn filter_ok (self: Self , f: F ) -> FilterOk Return an iterator adaptor that filters every Result::Ok value with the provided closure. Result::Err values are unchanged. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let input = vec! Ok(22), Err(false), Ok(11) ; let it = input.into_iter().filter_ok(|&i| i > 20); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! Ok(22), Err(false) ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let input = vec! [ Ok ( 22 ), Err ( false ), Ok ( 11 )]; let it = input . into_iter () . filter_ok ( |&#x26; i | i > 20 ); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [ Ok ( 22 ), Err ( false )]); Run Source filter_map_ok fn filter_map_ok (self: Self , f: F ) -> FilterMapOk Return an iterator adaptor that filters and transforms every Result::Ok value with the provided closure. Result::Err values are unchanged. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let input = vec! Ok(22), Err(false), Ok(11) ; let it = input.into_iter().filter_map_ok(|i| if i > 20 { Some(i * 2) } else { None }); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! Ok(44), Err(false) ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let input = vec! [ Ok ( 22 ), Err ( false ), Ok ( 11 )]; let it = input . into_iter () . filter_map_ok ( | i | if i > 20 { Some ( i * 2 ) } else { None }); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [ Ok ( 44 ), Err ( false )]); Run Source flatten_ok fn flatten_ok (self: Self ) -> FlattenOk Return an iterator adaptor that flattens every Result::Ok value into a series of Result::Ok values. Result::Err values are unchanged. This is useful when you have some common error type for your crate and need to propagate it upwards, but the Result::Ok case needs to be flattened. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let input = vec! Ok(0..2), Err(false), Ok(2..4) ; let it = input.iter().cloned().flatten_ok(); itertools::assert_equal(it.clone(), vec! Ok(0), Ok(1), Err(false), Ok(2), Ok(3) ); // This can also be used to propagate errors when collecting. let output_result: Result , bool> = it.collect(); assert_eq!(output_result, Err(false)); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let input = vec! [ Ok ( 0 .. 2 ), Err ( false ), Ok ( 2 .. 4 )]; let it = input . iter () . cloned () . flatten_ok (); itertools :: assert_equal ( it . clone (), vec! [ Ok ( 0 ), Ok ( 1 ), Err ( false ), Ok ( 2 ), Ok ( 3 )]); // This can also be used to propagate errors when collecting. let output_result : Result &#x3C; Vec &#x3C; i32 >, bool > = it . collect (); assert_eq! ( output_result , Err ( false )); Run Source process_results fn process_results (self: Self , processor: F ) -> Result “Lift” a function of the values of the current iterator so as to process an iterator of Result values instead. processor is a closure that receives an adapted version of the iterator as the only argument — the adapted iterator produces elements of type T , as long as the original iterator produces Ok values. If the original iterable produces an error at any point, the adapted iterator ends and it will return the error itself. Otherwise, the return value from the closure is returned wrapped inside Ok . Example Copy use itertools::Itertools; type Item = Result ; let first_values: Vec = vec! Ok(1), Ok(0), Ok(3) ; let second_values: Vec = vec! Ok(2), Ok(1), Err("overflow") ; // “Lift” the iterator .max() method to work on the Ok-values. let first_max = first_values.into_iter().process_results(|iter| iter.max().unwrap_or(0)); let second_max = second_values.into_iter().process_results(|iter| iter.max().unwrap_or(0)); assert_eq!(first_max, Ok(3)); assert!(second_max.is_err()); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; type Item = Result &#x3C; i32 , &#x26; ' static str >; let first_values : Vec &#x3C; Item > = vec! [ Ok ( 1 ), Ok ( 0 ), Ok ( 3 )]; let second_values : Vec &#x3C; Item > = vec! [ Ok ( 2 ), Ok ( 1 ), Err ( "overflow" )]; // “Lift” the iterator .max() method to work on the Ok-values. let first_max = first_values . into_iter () . process_results ( | iter | iter . max () . unwrap_or ( 0 )); let second_max = second_values . into_iter () . process_results ( | iter | iter . max () . unwrap_or ( 0 )); assert_eq! ( first_max , Ok ( 3 )); assert! ( second_max . is_err ()); Run Source merge fn merge (self: Self , other: J ) -> Merge ::IntoIter> Return an iterator adaptor that merges the two base iterators in ascending order. If both base iterators are sorted (ascending), the result is sorted. Iterator element type is Self::Item . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let a = (0..11).step_by(3); let b = (0..11).step_by(5); let it = a.merge(b); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! 0, 0, 3, 5, 6, 9, 10 ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let a = ( 0 .. 11 ) . step_by ( 3 ); let b = ( 0 .. 11 ) . step_by ( 5 ); let it = a . merge ( b ); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [ 0 , 0 , 3 , 5 , 6 , 9 , 10 ]); Run Source merge_by fn merge_by (self: Self , other: J , is_first: F ) -> MergeBy ::IntoIter, F> Return an iterator adaptor that merges the two base iterators in order. This is much like .merge() but allows for a custom ordering. This can be especially useful for sequences of tuples. Iterator element type is Self::Item . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let a = (0..).zip("bc".chars()); let b = (0..).zip("ad".chars()); let it = a.merge_by(b, |x, y| x.1 itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! (0, 'a'), (0, 'b'), (1, 'c'), (1, 'd') ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let a = ( 0 .. ) . zip ( "bc" . chars ()); let b = ( 0 .. ) . zip ( "ad" . chars ()); let it = a . merge_by ( b , | x , y | x . 1 &#x3C;= y . 1 ); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [( 0 , 'a' ), ( 0 , 'b' ), ( 1 , 'c' ), ( 1 , 'd' )]); Run Source merge_join_by fn merge_join_by (self: Self , other: J , cmp_fn: F ) -> MergeJoinBy ::IntoIter, F> Create an iterator that merges items from both this and the specified iterator in ascending order. The function can either return an Ordering variant or a boolean. If cmp_fn returns Ordering , it chooses whether to pair elements based on the Ordering returned by the specified compare function. At any point, inspecting the tip of the iterators I and J as items i of type I::Item and j of type J::Item respectively, the resulting iterator will: Emit EitherOrBoth::Left(i) when i , and remove i from its source iterator Emit EitherOrBoth::Right(j) when i > j , and remove j from its source iterator Emit EitherOrBoth::Both(i, j) when i == j , and remove both i and j from their respective source iterators Copy use itertools::EitherOrBoth::{Both, Left, Right}; use itertools::Itertools; let a = vec! 0, 2, 4, 6, 1 .into_iter(); let b = (0..10).step_by(3); itertools::assert_equal( // This performs a diff in the style of the Unix command comm(1), // generalized to arbitrary types rather than text. a.merge_join_by(b, Ord::cmp), vec! Both(0, 0), Left(2), Right(3), Left(4), Both(6, 6), Left(1), Right(9) ); If cmp_fn returns bool , it chooses whether to pair elements based on the boolean returned by the specified function. At any point, inspecting the tip of the iterators I and J as items i of type I::Item and j of type J::Item respectively, the resulting iterator will: Emit Either::Left(i) when true , and remove i from its source iterator Emit Either::Right(j) when false , and remove j from its source iterator It is similar to the Ordering case if the first argument is considered "less" than the second argument. Copy use itertools::Either::{Left, Right}; use itertools::Itertools; let a = vec! 0, 2, 4, 6, 1 .into_iter(); let b = (0..10).step_by(3); itertools::assert_equal( a.merge_join_by(b, |i, j| i vec! Left(0), Right(0), Left(2), Right(3), Left(4), Left(6), Left(1), Right(6), Right(9) ); Copy use itertools :: EitherOrBoth :: { Both , Left , Right }; use itertools :: Itertools ; let a = vec! [ 0 , 2 , 4 , 6 , 1 ] . into_iter (); let b = ( 0 .. 10 ) . step_by ( 3 ); itertools :: assert_equal ( // This performs a diff in the style of the Unix command comm(1), // generalized to arbitrary types rather than text. a . merge_join_by ( b , Ord :: cmp ), vec! [ Both ( 0 , 0 ), Left ( 2 ), Right ( 3 ), Left ( 4 ), Both ( 6 , 6 ), Left ( 1 ), Right ( 9 )] ); Run Copy use itertools :: Either :: { Left , Right }; use itertools :: Itertools ; let a = vec! [ 0 , 2 , 4 , 6 , 1 ] . into_iter (); let b = ( 0 .. 10 ) . step_by ( 3 ); itertools :: assert_equal ( a . merge_join_by ( b , | i , j | i &#x3C;= j ), vec! [ Left ( 0 ), Right ( 0 ), Left ( 2 ), Right ( 3 ), Left ( 4 ), Left ( 6 ), Left ( 1 ), Right ( 6 ), Right ( 9 )] ); Run Source kmerge fn kmerge (self: Self ) -> KMerge ::Item as IntoIterator>::IntoIter> Return an iterator adaptor that flattens an iterator of iterators by merging them in ascending order. Duplicates are preserved. If all base iterators are sorted (ascending), the result is sorted. Iterator element type is Self::Item . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let a = (0..6).step_by(3); // 0, 3 let b = (1..6).step_by(2); // 1, 3, 5 let c = (2..6).step_by(3); // 2, 5 let it = vec! a, b, c .into_iter().kmerge(); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! 0, 1, 2, 3, 3, 5, 5 ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let a = ( 0 .. 6 ) . step_by ( 3 ); // [0, 3] let b = ( 1 .. 6 ) . step_by ( 2 ); // [1, 3, 5 ] let c = ( 2 .. 6 ) . step_by ( 3 ); // [2, 5] let it = vec! [ a , b , c ] . into_iter () . kmerge (); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [ 0 , 1 , 2 , 3 , 3 , 5 , 5 ]); Run Source kmerge_by fn kmerge_by (self: Self , first: F ) -> KMergeBy ::Item as IntoIterator>::IntoIter, F> Return an iterator adaptor that flattens an iterator of iterators by merging them according to the given closure. The closure first is called with two elements a , b and should return true if a is ordered before b . If all base iterators are sorted according to first , the result is sorted. Iterator element type is Self::Item . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let a = vec! -1f64, 2., 3., -5., 6., -7. ; let b = vec! 0., 2., -4. ; let mut it = vec! a, b .into_iter().kmerge_by(|a, b| a.abs() assert_eq!(it.next(), Some(0.)); assert_eq!(it.last(), Some(-7.)); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let a = vec! [ - 1 f64 , 2 . , 3 . , - 5 . , 6 . , - 7 . ]; let b = vec! [ 0 . , 2 . , - 4 . ]; let mut it = vec! [ a , b ] . into_iter () . kmerge_by ( | a , b | a . abs () &#x3C; b . abs ()); assert_eq! ( it . next (), Some ( 0 . )); assert_eq! ( it . last (), Some ( - 7 . )); Run Source cartesian_product fn cartesian_product (self: Self , other: J ) -> Product ::IntoIter> Return an iterator adaptor that iterates over the cartesian product of the element sets of two iterators self and J . Iterator element type is (Self::Item, J::Item) . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let it = (0..2).cartesian_product("αβ".chars()); itertools::assert_equal(it, vec! (0, 'α'), (0, 'β'), (1, 'α'), (1, 'β') ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let it = ( 0 .. 2 ) . cartesian_product ( "αβ" . chars ()); itertools :: assert_equal ( it , vec! [( 0 , 'α' ), ( 0 , 'β' ), ( 1 , 'α' ), ( 1 , 'β' )]); Run Source multi_cartesian_product fn multi_cartesian_product (self: Self ) -> MultiProduct ::Item as IntoIterator>::IntoIter> Return an iterator adaptor that iterates over the cartesian product of all subiterators returned by meta-iterator self . All provided iterators must yield the same Item type. To generate the product of iterators yielding multiple types, use the iproduct macro instead. The iterator element type is Vec , where T is the iterator element of the subiterators. Note that the iterator is fused. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let mut multi_prod = (0..3).map(|i| (i * 2)..(i * 2 + 2)) .multi_cartesian_product(); assert_eq!(multi_prod.next(), Some(vec! 0, 2, 4 )); assert_eq!(multi_prod.next(), Some(vec! 0, 2, 5 )); assert_eq!(multi_prod.next(), Some(vec! 0, 3, 4 )); assert_eq!(multi_prod.next(), Some(vec! 0, 3, 5 )); assert_eq!(multi_prod.next(), Some(vec! 1, 2, 4 )); assert_eq!(multi_prod.next(), Some(vec! 1, 2, 5 )); assert_eq!(multi_prod.next(), Some(vec! 1, 3, 4 )); assert_eq!(multi_prod.next(), Some(vec! 1, 3, 5 )); assert_eq!(multi_prod.next(), None); If the adapted iterator is empty, the result is an iterator yielding a single empty vector. This is known as the nullary cartesian product . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let mut nullary_cartesian_product = (0..0).map(|i| (i * 2)..(i * 2 + 2)).multi_cartesian_product(); assert_eq!(nullary_cartesian_product.next(), Some(vec![])); assert_eq!(nullary_cartesian_product.next(), None); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let mut multi_prod = ( 0 .. 3 ) . map ( | i | ( i * 2 ) .. ( i * 2 + 2 )) . multi_cartesian_product (); assert_eq! ( multi_prod . next (), Some ( vec! [ 0 , 2 , 4 ])); assert_eq! ( multi_prod . next (), Some ( vec! [ 0 , 2 , 5 ])); assert_eq! ( multi_prod . next (), Some ( vec! [ 0 , 3 , 4 ])); assert_eq! ( multi_prod . next (), Some ( vec! [ 0 , 3 , 5 ])); assert_eq! ( multi_prod . next (), Some ( vec! [ 1 , 2 , 4 ])); assert_eq! ( multi_prod . next (), Some ( vec! [ 1 , 2 , 5 ])); assert_eq! ( multi_prod . next (), Some ( vec! [ 1 , 3 , 4 ])); assert_eq! ( multi_prod . next (), Some ( vec! [ 1 , 3 , 5 ])); assert_eq! ( multi_prod . next (), None ); Run Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let mut nullary_cartesian_product = ( 0 .. 0 ) . map ( | i | ( i * 2 ) .. ( i * 2 + 2 )) . multi_cartesian_product (); assert_eq! ( nullary_cartesian_product . next (), Some ( vec! [])); assert_eq! ( nullary_cartesian_product . next (), None ); Run Source coalesce fn coalesce (self: Self , f: F ) -> Coalesce Return an iterator adaptor that uses the passed-in closure to optionally merge together consecutive elements. The closure f is passed two elements, previous and current and may return either (1) Ok(combined) to merge the two values or (2) Err((previous', current')) to indicate they can't be merged. In (2), the value previous' is emitted by the iterator. Either (1) combined or (2) current' becomes the previous value when coalesce continues with the next pair of elements to merge. The value that remains at the end is also emitted by the iterator. Iterator element type is Self::Item . This iterator is fused . Copy use itertools::Itertools; // sum same-sign runs together let data = vec! -1., -2., -3., 3., 1., 0., -1. ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().coalesce(|x, y| if (x >= 0.) == (y >= 0.) { Ok(x + y) } else { Err((x, y)) }), vec! -6., 4., -1. ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; // sum same-sign runs together let data = vec! [ - 1 . , - 2 . , - 3 . , 3 . , 1 . , 0 . , - 1 . ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . coalesce ( | x , y | if ( x >= 0 . ) == ( y >= 0 . ) { Ok ( x + y ) } else { Err (( x , y )) }), vec! [ - 6 . , 4 . , - 1 . ]); Run Source dedup fn dedup (self: Self ) -> Dedup Remove duplicates from sections of consecutive identical elements. If the iterator is sorted, all elements will be unique. Iterator element type is Self::Item . This iterator is fused . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! 1., 1., 2., 3., 3., 2., 2. ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().dedup(), vec! 1., 2., 3., 2. ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ 1 . , 1 . , 2 . , 3 . , 3 . , 2 . , 2 . ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . dedup (), vec! [ 1 . , 2 . , 3 . , 2 . ]); Run Source dedup_by fn dedup_by (self: Self , cmp: Cmp ) -> DedupBy Remove duplicates from sections of consecutive identical elements, determining equality using a comparison function. If the iterator is sorted, all elements will be unique. Iterator element type is Self::Item . This iterator is fused . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! (0, 1.), (1, 1.), (0, 2.), (0, 3.), (1, 3.), (1, 2.), (2, 2.) ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().dedup_by(|x, y| x.1 == y.1), vec! (0, 1.), (0, 2.), (0, 3.), (1, 2.) ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [( 0 , 1 . ), ( 1 , 1 . ), ( 0 , 2 . ), ( 0 , 3 . ), ( 1 , 3 . ), ( 1 , 2 . ), ( 2 , 2 . )]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . dedup_by ( | x , y | x . 1 == y . 1 ), vec! [( 0 , 1 . ), ( 0 , 2 . ), ( 0 , 3 . ), ( 1 , 2 . )]); Run Source dedup_with_count fn dedup_with_count (self: Self ) -> DedupWithCount Remove duplicates from sections of consecutive identical elements, while keeping a count of how many repeated elements were present. If the iterator is sorted, all elements will be unique. Iterator element type is (usize, Self::Item) . This iterator is fused . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! 'a', 'a', 'b', 'c', 'c', 'b', 'b' ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().dedup_with_count(), vec! (2, 'a'), (1, 'b'), (2, 'c'), (2, 'b') ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ 'a' , 'a' , 'b' , 'c' , 'c' , 'b' , 'b' ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . dedup_with_count (), vec! [( 2 , 'a' ), ( 1 , 'b' ), ( 2 , 'c' ), ( 2 , 'b' )]); Run Source dedup_by_with_count fn dedup_by_with_count (self: Self , cmp: Cmp ) -> DedupByWithCount Remove duplicates from sections of consecutive identical elements, while keeping a count of how many repeated elements were present. This will determine equality using a comparison function. If the iterator is sorted, all elements will be unique. Iterator element type is (usize, Self::Item) . This iterator is fused . Copy use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! (0, 'a'), (1, 'a'), (0, 'b'), (0, 'c'), (1, 'c'), (1, 'b'), (2, 'b') ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().dedup_by_with_count(|x, y| x.1 == y.1), vec! (2, (0, 'a')), (1, (0, 'b')), (2, (0, 'c')), (2, (1, 'b')) ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [( 0 , 'a' ), ( 1 , 'a' ), ( 0 , 'b' ), ( 0 , 'c' ), ( 1 , 'c' ), ( 1 , 'b' ), ( 2 , 'b' )]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . dedup_by_with_count ( | x , y | x . 1 == y . 1 ), vec! [( 2 , ( 0 , 'a' )), ( 1 , ( 0 , 'b' )), ( 2 , ( 0 , 'c' )), ( 2 , ( 1 , 'b' ))]); Run Source duplicates fn duplicates (self: Self ) -> Duplicates Return an iterator adaptor that produces elements that appear more than once during the iteration. Duplicates are detected using hash and equality. The iterator is stable, returning the duplicate items in the order in which they occur in the adapted iterator. Each duplicate item is returned exactly once. If an item appears more than twice, the second item is the item retained and the rest are discarded. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! 10, 20, 30, 20, 40, 10, 50 ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().duplicates(), vec! 20, 10 ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ 10 , 20 , 30 , 20 , 40 , 10 , 50 ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . duplicates (), vec! [ 20 , 10 ]); Run Source duplicates_with_hasher fn duplicates_with_hasher (self: Self , hash_builder: S ) -> Duplicates Return an iterator which yields the same elements as the one returned by .duplicates(), but uses the specified hash builder to hash the elements for comparison. Warning: hash_builder is normally randomly generated, and is designed to allow it's users to be resistant to attacks that cause many collisions and very poor performance. Setting it manually using this function can expose a DoS attack vector. Copy use std::hash::RandomState; use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! 10, 20, 30, 20, 40, 10, 50 ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().duplicates_with_hasher(RandomState::new()), vec! 20,10 ); Copy use std :: hash :: RandomState ; use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ 10 , 20 , 30 , 20 , 40 , 10 , 50 ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . duplicates_with_hasher ( RandomState :: new ()), vec! [ 20 , 10 ]); Run Source duplicates_by fn duplicates_by (self: Self , f: F ) -> DuplicatesBy Return an iterator adaptor that produces elements that appear more than once during the iteration. Duplicates are detected using hash and equality. Duplicates are detected by comparing the key they map to with the keying function f by hash and equality. The keys are stored in a hash map in the iterator. The iterator is stable, returning the duplicate items in the order in which they occur in the adapted iterator. Each duplicate item is returned exactly once. If an item appears more than twice, the second item is the item retained and the rest are discarded. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! "a", "bb", "aa", "c", "ccc" ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().duplicates_by(|s| s.len()), vec! "aa", "c" ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ "a" , "bb" , "aa" , "c" , "ccc" ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . duplicates_by ( | s | s . len ()), vec! [ "aa" , "c" ]); Run Source duplicates_by_with_hasher fn duplicates_by_with_hasher (self: Self , f: F , hash_builder: S ) -> DuplicatesBy Return an iterator which yields the same elements as the one returned by .duplicates_by(), but uses the specified hash builder to hash the keys for comparison. Warning: hash_builder is normally randomly generated, and is designed to allow it's users to be resistant to attacks that cause many collisions and very poor performance. Setting it manually using this function can expose a DoS attack vector. Copy use std::hash::RandomState; use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! "a", "bb", "aa", "c", "ccc" ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().duplicates_by_with_hasher(|s| s.len(),RandomState::new()), vec! "aa", "c" ); Copy use std :: hash :: RandomState ; use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ "a" , "bb" , "aa" , "c" , "ccc" ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . duplicates_by_with_hasher ( | s | s . len (), RandomState :: new ()), vec! [ "aa" , "c" ]); Run Source unique fn unique (self: Self ) -> Unique Return an iterator adaptor that filters out elements that have already been produced once during the iteration. Duplicates are detected using hash and equality. Clones of visited elements are stored in a hash set in the iterator. The iterator is stable, returning the non-duplicate items in the order in which they occur in the adapted iterator. In a set of duplicate items, the first item encountered is the item retained. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! 10, 20, 30, 20, 40, 10, 50 ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().unique(), vec! 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ 10 , 20 , 30 , 20 , 40 , 10 , 50 ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . unique (), vec! [ 10 , 20 , 30 , 40 , 50 ]); Run Source unique_with_hasher fn unique_with_hasher (self: Self , hash_builder: S ) -> Unique Return an iterator which yields the same elements as the one returned by .unique(), but uses the specified hash builder to hash the elements for comparison. Warning: hash_builder is normally randomly generated, and is designed to allow it's users to be resistant to attacks that cause many collisions and very poor performance. Setting it manually using this function can expose a DoS attack vector. Copy use std::hash::RandomState; use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! 10, 20, 30, 20, 40, 10, 50 ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().unique_with_hasher(RandomState::new()), vec! 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 ); Copy use std :: hash :: RandomState ; use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ 10 , 20 , 30 , 20 , 40 , 10 , 50 ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . unique_with_hasher ( RandomState :: new ()), vec! [ 10 , 20 , 30 , 40 , 50 ]); Run Source unique_by fn unique_by (self: Self , f: F ) -> UniqueBy Return an iterator adaptor that filters out elements that have already been produced once during the iteration. Duplicates are detected by comparing the key they map to with the keying function f by hash and equality. The keys are stored in a hash set in the iterator. The iterator is stable, returning the non-duplicate items in the order in which they occur in the adapted iterator. In a set of duplicate items, the first item encountered is the item retained. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! "a", "bb", "aa", "c", "ccc" ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().unique_by(|s| s.len()), vec! "a", "bb", "ccc" ); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ "a" , "bb" , "aa" , "c" , "ccc" ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . unique_by ( | s | s . len ()), vec! [ "a" , "bb" , "ccc" ]); Run Source unique_by_with_hasher fn unique_by_with_hasher (self: Self , f: F , hash_builder: S ) -> UniqueBy Return an iterator which yields the same elements as the one returned by .unique_by(), but uses the specified hash builder to hash the elements for comparison. Warning: hash_builder is normally randomly generated, and is designed to allow it's users to be resistant to attacks that cause many collisions and very poor performance. Setting it manually using this function can expose a DoS attack vector. Copy use std::hash::RandomState; use itertools::Itertools; let data = vec! "a", "bb", "aa", "c", "ccc" ; itertools::assert_equal(data.into_iter().unique_by_with_hasher(|s| s.len(), RandomState::new()), vec! "a", "bb", "ccc" ); Copy use std :: hash :: RandomState ; use itertools :: Itertools ; let data = vec! [ "a" , "bb" , "aa" , "c" , "ccc" ]; itertools :: assert_equal ( data . into_iter () . unique_by_with_hasher ( | s | s . len (), RandomState :: new ()), vec! [ "a" , "bb" , "ccc" ]); Run Source peeking_take_while fn peeking_take_while (self: &mut Self , accept: F ) -> PeekingTakeWhile Return an iterator adaptor that borrows from this iterator and takes items while the closure accept returns true . This adaptor can only be used on iterators that implement PeekingNext like .peekable() , put_back and a few other collection iterators. The last and rejected element (first false ) is still available when peeking_take_while is done. See also .take_while_ref() which is a similar adaptor. Source take_while_ref fn take_while_ref (self: &mut Self , accept: F ) -> TakeWhileRef Return an iterator adaptor that borrows from a Clone -able iterator to only pick off elements while the predicate accept returns true . It uses the Clone trait to restore the original iterator so that the last and rejected element (first false ) is still available when take_while_ref is done. Copy use itertools::Itertools; let mut hexadecimals = "0123456789abcdef".chars(); let decimals = hexadecimals.take_while_ref(|c| c.is_numeric()) .collect:: (); assert_eq!(decimals, "0123456789"); assert_eq!(hexadecimals.next(), Some('a')); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let mut hexadecimals = "0123456789abcdef" . chars (); let decimals = hexadecimals . take_while_ref ( | c | c . is_numeric ()) . collect :: &#x3C; String >(); assert_eq! ( decimals , "0123456789" ); assert_eq! ( hexadecimals . next (), Some ( 'a' )); Run Source take_while_inclusive fn take_while_inclusive (self: Self , accept: F ) -> TakeWhileInclusive Returns an iterator adaptor that consumes elements while the given predicate is true , including the element for which the predicate first returned false . The .take_while() std::iter::Iterator::take_while adaptor is useful when you want items satisfying a predicate, but to know when to stop taking elements, we have to consume that first element that doesn't satisfy the predicate. This adaptor includes that element where .take_while() std::iter::Iterator::take_while would drop it. The .take_while_ref() crate::Itertools::take_while_ref adaptor serves a similar purpose, but this adaptor doesn't require Clone ing the underlying elements. Copy # use itertools :: Itertools ; let items = vec! code title = "unresolved intra-doc link" > 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 / code >; let filtered : Vec _ > = items . into_iter () . take_while_inclusive ( |& n | n % 3 != 0 ) . collect (); assert_eq! ( filtered , vec! code title = "unresolved intra-doc link" > 1 , 2 , 3 / code >); Copy # use itertools :: Itertools ; let items = vec! code title = "unresolved intra-doc link" > 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 / code >; let take_while_inclusive_result : Vec _ > = items . iter () . copied () . take_while_inclusive ( |& n | n % 3 != 0 ) . collect (); let take_while_result : Vec _ > = items . into_iter () . take_while ( |& n | n % 3 != 0 ) . collect (); assert_eq! ( take_while_inclusive_result , vec! code title = "unresolved intra-doc link" > 1 , 2 , 3 / code >); assert_eq! ( take_while_result , vec! code title = "unresolved intra-doc link" > 1 , 2 / code >); // both iterators have the same items remaining at this point---the 3 // is lost from the `take_while` vec Copy # use itertools :: Itertools ; # code title = "unresolved intra-doc link" > derive ( Debug , PartialEq ) / code > struct NoCloneImpl ( i32 ); let non_cloneable_items : Vec _ > = vec! code title = "unresolved intra-doc link" > 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 / code > . into_iter () . map ( NoCloneImpl ) . collect (); let filtered : Vec _ > = non_cloneable_items . into_iter () . take_while_inclusive ( | n | n . 0 % 3 != 0 ) . collect (); let expected : Vec _ > = vec! code title = "unresolved intra-doc link" > 1 , 2 , 3 / code > . into_iter () . map ( NoCloneImpl ) . collect (); assert_eq! ( filtered , expected ); Copy let items = vec! [ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 ]; let filtered : Vec &#x3C; _ > = items . into_iter () . take_while_inclusive ( |&#x26; n | n % 3 != 0 ) . collect (); assert_eq! ( filtered , vec! [ 1 , 2 , 3 ]); Copy use itertools :: Itertools ; let items = vec! [ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 ]; let filtered : Vec &#x3C; _ > = items . into_iter () . take_while_inclusive ( |&#x26; n | n % 3 != 0 ) . collect (); assert_eq! ( filtered , vec! [ 1 , 2 , 3 ]); Run Show hidden lines Copy let items = vec! [ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 ]; let take_while_inclusive_result : Vec &#x3C; _ > = items . iter () . copied () . take_while_inclusive ( |&#x26; n | n % 3 != 0 ) . collect (); let take_while_result : Vec &#x3C; _ > = items . into_iter () . take_while ( |&#x26; n | n % 3 != 0 ) . collect (); assert_eq! ( take_while_inclusive_result , vec! [ 1 , 2 , 3 ]); assert_eq! ( take_while_result , vec!
